query: radiosensitivity of organs in pelvic irradiation

retrieved_docs.title: Adverse Effects of Radiation Therapy -- Clinical Significance -- Abdomen and Pelvis
retrieved_docs.content: Gonads - Irradiation to ovaries leads to infertility or premature ovarian failure even at low doses with increased sensitivity with advancing age. For women under 40 with a strong desire to preserve fertility, the ovarian transposition procedure can reduce the risk of irradiation. Long-term management is a hormone replacement therapy for menopausal symptoms. Radiotherapy may result in impotence and testicular dysfunction in males. Patients undergoing radiotherapy should be offered sperm or egg cryopreservation options before undergoing RT.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Exposure In Pregnancy -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: Understanding the anatomic location of the uterus is essential in understanding why a particular type of study contributes to a higher radiation dose. The uterus is located within the female pelvis. Therefore, studies that are further away from the pelvis contribute to less radiation dose than the studies that aim at the pelvis. Additionally, the uterus is located superior and anterior aspect of the pelvis during the pregnancy. X-ray beams that project in a posterior to anterior (PA) direction contribute to less radiation than the beam projected in anterior to posterior (AP) direction because, in PA projection, the X-ray gets attenuated before reaching anteriorly located uterus.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- BT dose and dose constraints
retrieved_docs.content: Ensuring adequate dose to the target is critical and has been shown to lead to improved local control and survival. Dose and fractionation schemes may vary by institutional preference, but they must result in a total equivalent dose in 2 Gy fractions (EQD2) of 85 Gy or higher, assuming 45 Gy was initially delivered to the pelvis. There are several calculators to determine EQD2. One of these, colloquially known as the “Vienna Spreadsheet,” was used by the Effects of a Multifaceted Intervention on Blood Pressure Actions in the Primary Care Environment (EMBRACE) trial group; it provides EQD2 calculations to the target structures and OAR. The most common dose fractionation schemes include 4 x 7 Gy, 5 x 6 Gy, and 6 x 5 Gy, with an EQD2 of approximately 90.1, 88.6, and 83.7 Gy, respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Clinical Significance -- Fertility Issues in Radiotherapy Patients
retrieved_docs.content: Cranial or head/neck radiation can damage the hypothalamic-pituitary axis affecting patient fertility since head and neck cancers require high doses in the 40 to 70 Gy range. Direct radiation to the ovaries can also cause early ovarian failure. As described earlier, pelvis radiation may cause structural changes such as reduced uterine volume, lack of endometrial response to estrogen, or impaired uterine artery blood flow, impeding successful embryonal implantation or development. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominopelvic plain radiography – When the fetus is directly in the field of view, these techniques can be employed to minimize fetal radiation exposure: Doing a posterior-anterior (PA) exposure reduces the fetal radiation dose by 0.02 to 0.04 mGy (0.00002 to 0.00004 Gy) when compared with the traditional anterior-posterior (AP) exposure, since the uterus is located in an anterior pelvic position. Shutters can be used to collimate the radiation beam to reduce scatter. Avoiding near uterus magnification and using grids decreases fetal exposure. Avoid repeat examinations

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Proctitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation proctitis occurs as a result of radiation therapy for malignancy to organs in the pelvis, including the prostate, rectum, and reproductive organs. The degree of radiation proctitis varies, depending on the modality by which the radiation is delivered, the radiation dose, and the volume of irradiated tissue. Several specific risk factors have been identified, such as a dose of radiation to the rectum >45 Gy, a history of inflammatory bowel disease, HIV/AIDS, and a possible genetic predisposition. [2] [3] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Treatment of Radiation Proctitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation therapy of the pelvis is used to treat a variety of cancers, for example, ovarian, uterine, cervical, prostate, bladder, and rectal cancers. Radiation damage to the gastrointestinal (GI) tract can occur, yet the treatment is beneficial in fighting cancer. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy combats this otherwise difficult-to-treat complication of radiation therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Clinical Significance -- Cervical Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: For locally advanced stages, including stage 1B2 tumors (tumors greater than 4 cm), recommended treatment involves radical radiotherapy for 5.5 weeks with 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with simultaneous chemotherapy followed by intracavity brachytherapy. Such high radiation doses imply that therapeutic radiotherapy and pregnancy are incompatible.

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Effects on the Fetus -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The American Association of Physicists in Medicine and the International Commission on Radiological Protection described guidelines for assessing the potential fetal radiation exposure during maternal radiotherapy. They recognized three possible radiation sources that need to be evaluated: first, the photon leakage from the machine head; second, the scatter and leakage from the collimators and beam modifiers; and lastly, scattered radiation emerging from the treatment beams of the volume treated within the patient. To reduce the leakage and scatter from the treatment head, beam modifiers, and collimators, lead shielding is placed on the pregnant mother's abdomen and pelvis. Shielding is possible earlier in the pregnancy, but as the gravid uterus grows, it is difficult for adequate shielding. Also, as the abdominal size increases, the distance between the field and fetus reduces, thus increasing the risk of exposure while treating supradiaphragmatic areas. In this circumstance, the fetus receives 10 to 15 times more radiation dose for the same treatment field.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- ERBT dosing and dose constraints
retrieved_docs.content: If BT is planned, minimizing these doses during the external beam phase of treatment will allow higher doses to be delivered during the boost phase of treatment. Bone marrow suppression resulting in grades 3 to 4 neutropenia has been demonstrably lower (8.6% vs 27.1%) with image-guided radiotherapy and IMRT. [56] The most common constraints are for bone marrow in the pelvis. [56] [57] [61]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Anal Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- Dose Constraints
retrieved_docs.content: The main purpose of dose constraints is to limit the acute and long-term toxicities associated with radiation to the pelvis when using IMRT. RTOG 0529 limited small and large bowel to V45 Gy less than 20 ccs, V35 Gy less than 150 cc, and V30 Gy less than 200 ccs. This resulted in acute grade 2+ GI adverse events of 73% and acute grade 3+ GI adverse events of 21%. The same study limited bladder dose to V50 Gy <= 5%, V40 Gy less than or equal to 35%, and V35 Gy less than or equal to 50%. These constraints limited acute grade 2+ GU adverse events to 15% and acute grade 3+ GU adverse events to 2%. [31] The overall rate of late GI grade 2+ adverse events was 10%, and late GU grade 2+ adverse events were 4%. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- Secondary malignancy
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation-induced cancers tend to appear several decades after treatment. The overall risk of secondary malignancy is increased with pelvic radiotherapy compared to those treated with surgery alone. Women younger than 50 years had a 40-year cumulative risk of 22% versus 16% for those older than 50 years. [74] Secondary cancers tend to be confined to the rectum, bladder, lungs, and genitals. Other retrospective studies have shown an increase in leukemia risk, which peaks around 5 to 10 years after treatment. [75] Limiting normal organ doses may help to reduce the risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- BT dose and dose constraints
retrieved_docs.content: Doses to OAR must also be carefully documented. Again, it is the cumulative dose of EQD2 that is critical. The dosimetric parameter D2 cc, which represents the highest dose received by 2 ccs of tissue, is commonly used for evaluating a BT plan. The American Brachytherapy Society (ABS) guidelines allow for the D2 cc of the bladder to receive 90 Gy or less EQD2, whereas the D2 cc of the rectum and sigmoid should be 75 Gy or less EQD2. However, recent data suggest that late rectal morbidity may be substantially lower even using a D2 cc of 65 Gy or less. [70]

retrieved_docs.title: Brachytherapy -- Clinical Significance -- Prostate Brachytherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Prostate brachytherapy features prominently in the treatment of prostate cancer. It may be used as monotherapy in low and favorable intermediate-risk prostate cancer or as a boost when combined with external beam radiotherapy in high-risk disease. In addition, some centers have used brachytherapy for salvage therapy in recurrent cases. LDR and HDR modalities have both been utilized with excellent outcomes. Contraindications to prostate brachytherapy include distant metastasis, unacceptable operative risk, large prostate urethral defects, ataxia telangiectasia, absence of rectum, and limited life expectancy. [4] Relative contraindications include prostate gland size greater than 60 ccs, IPSS scores greater than 20, inflammatory bowel disease, and large median prostate lobe.

retrieved_docs.title: Adverse Effects of Radiation Therapy -- Clinical Significance -- Abdomen and Pelvis
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrointestinal System - Acute radiation toxicity presents as anorexia, nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps, and diarrhea about 2-3 weeks after radiation therapy. Radiation injury to the large bowel presents with large volume watery diarrhea. Chronic effects include chronic diarrhea, malabsorption, recurrent bouts of ileus or obstruction, proliferative mucosal telangiectasias, or ulceration. The rectum is the most commonly affected normal tissue in radiotherapy for prostate and cervical cancer. Symptoms of acute radiation injury are diarrhea, increased mucus secretion, and tenesmus due to loss of mucosal epithelium. Long-term complications are increased stool frequency, urgency, rectal bleeding, pain, variable degrees of incontinence and strictures, and fistula formation. [27] Treatment strategies include oral anti-inflammatory agents, analgesics, stool softeners, steroid enema, blood transfusions (for bleeding), and mechanical dilatation of strictures. For severe or refractory complications, hyperbaric oxygen, endoscopic or surgical intervention involving colostomy may be necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Radiation Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Historically, whole abdomen radiation was used in an attempt to treat ovarian cancer; however, due to ineffective results and increased frequency of toxicity and complications, this modality is no longer used for this indication. Currently, the role of radiation in ovarian cancer is limited to palliation, either for symptom control or to treat a localized spread of the disease. Adjuvant radiotherapy has not shown any survival benefit in the early stages of clear cell carcinoma and high-risk disease, as ovarian cancer typically does not remain confined to the pelvis. [61] [62]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2692
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is recognized as an allowable therapeutic option for both female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and uterine endometriosis (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). These two gynecologic conditions are interrelated, as uterine endometriosis is a known contributor to infertility in women. Consequently, radiotherapy may be considered within the broader management strategies for these linked disorders, reflecting their clinical connection and the potential role of radiotherapeutic interventions in addressing both conditions.

retrieved_docs.content: these compounds function as “allowed qualifiers” in the context of ionizing radiation because they can modify biological responses to radiation—either enhancing radiation‑induced damage, altering tissue radiosensitivity, or providing radioprotective or radiosensitizing effects. Awareness of these dual roles informs the integration of the agents into diagnostic imaging, radiotherapy planning, occupational radiation safety and broader radioprotection strategies, thereby optimizing therapeutic outcomes and patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: In radiation biology, each qualifier can modulate, enhance, mitigate, or otherwise influence physiological responses to ionizing and non‑ionizing radiation. Hypoxanthine’s PARP‑inhibitory activity specifically suppresses DNA‑repair mechanisms, affecting cellular recovery after radiation‑induced damage. Recognizing these agents as radiation modifiers informs the design of protective strategies, therapeutic regimens, and risk assessments for both therapeutic radiation exposure and accidental radiation incidents. Collectively, the inclusion of these permissible qualifiers facilitates standardized manufacturing, regulatory compliance, targeted therapeutic development, and a comprehensive understanding of immunological, metabolic, and radiobiological processes, thereby improving patient care and advancing biomedical research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1719
retrieved_docs.content: Infertility in women of cervical origin is classified as a specific type of cervical disorder (اضطراب في عنق الرحم). This condition is a subset of the broader category of female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث). Female infertility is closely related to endometrial implantation abnormalities (انتباذ بطاني رحمي), indicating that disturbances in the uterine lining can contribute to the inability to conceive. Radiotherapy (radioterapie) is recognized as an approved therapeutic option for cervical disorders and for endometrial implantation abnormalities; in certain clinical scenarios where these pathologies underlie female infertility, radiotherapy may be considered as part of the management strategy. Understanding the hierarchical relationship between cervical‑origin infertility, general female infertility, and endometrial implantation, as well as the role of radiotherapy, helps clinicians tailor comprehensive treatment plans for patients experiencing infertility.

This ultrasound examination serves as the methodological basis for both “تصوير البطن بالموجات فوق الصوتية” (abdominal ultrasound imaging) and “Ultrasonography guided insertion of catheter,” indicating that the same imaging technique is employed to obtain diagnostic images of the abdomen and to guide catheter placement safely and accurately.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Infertility in women of cervical origin is classified as a disorder of the cervix and is recognized as a specific subtype of female infertility. Radiotherapy is an allowed therapeutic qualifier for disorders of the cervix, female infertility, endometrial implantation, and mitral valve stenosis, and it can be qualified by a surgeon (قساح). Moreover, female infertility is related to endometrial implantation, indicating a pathophysiological connection between these reproductive conditions. This integrated view underscores how cervical‑origin infertility fits within broader gynecologic disorders and how radiotherapy, potentially overseen by a surgeon, may be applied across both gynecologic and cardiac conditions.

retrieved_docs.content: Intrakavitární radioterapie is a specialized form of radiation therapy that targets lesions within body cavities; the two terms are closely related and may be considered synonymous in clinical practice, reflecting the same therapeutic approach of delivering ionizing radiation to treat intracavitary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2696
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal pain, radiotherapy, and malignant hypertension are interrelated clinical concepts. Abdominal pain is recognized as a symptom that can be clinically associated with malignant hypertension, indicating that patients with severe, uncontrolled high blood pressure may present with abdominal discomfort. In addition, abdominal pain may arise as a qualifying symptom during radiotherapy, reflecting a possible side effect or concurrent condition in patients undergoing this treatment. Radiotherapy itself is also linked to malignant hypertension, suggesting that exposure to radiation can contribute to the development or exacerbation of severe hypertension. Together, these relationships highlight the importance of monitoring abdominal pain both as a potential indicator of malignant hypertension and as a possible adverse effect of radiotherapy, while also recognizing that radiotherapy may influence blood pressure regulation in susceptible individuals.

Overall, the hierarchy connects general radioactive‑isotope examinations to specific imaging and therapeutic techniques: transmission imaging and myocardial imaging derive from the broad radioactive‑isotope examination; nuclear medicine diagnostic procedures branch into radionuclide localization of tumor and radioisotope studies of the central nervous system; radionuclide imaging actions serve as methods for these procedures; and therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine link diagnostic imaging to radiation‑based treatments such as radiation therapy, intracavitary radiotherapy, and stereotactic radiation surgery. This integrated framework clarifies how diagnostic nuclear‑medicine modalities and therapeutic radiation practices are interrelated, supporting comprehensive patient evaluation and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2476
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an organ encompasses a variety of anatomical structures, including gonads and lymph nodes, reflecting the broad classification of bodily tissues that perform distinct physiological functions. In clinical practice, organs may be the focus of several procedural interventions. A vaginal biopsy (خزعة المهبل) can involve the excision of organ tissue for diagnostic evaluation, while pelvic cavity endoscopy (تنظير جوف الحوض) and skin grafting (طُعم الجلد) target organ structures as part of therapeutic or investigative procedures. Understanding that organs can represent both gonadal and lymphatic components, and recognizing the specific procedures that excise or target these tissues, is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and comprehensive patient care.
